title: Voices in the Mist
synopsis: When thick fog envelops a ferry crossing and Chloe becomes separated from her family, she and the other passengers must navigate through mysterious voices and shifting stories to find their way back to each other—and to the truth.

chapters.title: Day One: The Ferry's Call
chapters.content:
We heard the ferry's horn through the morning stillness before we saw the boat itself emerge from the fog.

Our families had planned this lake crossing for weeks—a simple day trip to visit the historic lighthouse on Beacon Island. The weatherman had promised clear skies, but nature had other plans. Thick fog rolled across Mirror Lake like a living thing, swallowing the shoreline and turning the world into something soft and strange.

"All aboard!" called Captain Morrison, his voice carrying across the water with practiced authority. His weathered hands gestured toward the gangplank as we shuffled forward—twelve passengers in total, plus our families.

Chloe bounced on her toes beside us, her short wavy strawberry-blonde hair catching what little light filtered through the mist. Her blue-gray eyes sparkled with excitement rather than concern about the weather. She had always been cheerful, finding wonder in unexpected places, though her enthusiasm sometimes led to stumbled steps and bumped elbows.

"Look how the fog makes everything mysterious," she whispered to us as we climbed aboard. "It's like we're sailing into a fairy tale."

The ferry's deck felt solid beneath our feet, but the fog pressed close around us, muffling sounds and softening edges. We could barely see ten feet in any direction. The other passengers moved like shadows—Mrs. Chen clutching her thermos of tea, the Patterson twins whispering together, old Mr. Bramble tapping his walking stick against the deck railing.

"Don't worry," Captain Morrison announced as the engine rumbled to life. "I've been crossing this lake for thirty years. Fog or no fog, we'll reach Beacon Island safe and sound."

But as the ferry pulled away from the dock, we noticed something odd. Voices drifted through the mist—not from other boats or the shore, but from somewhere else entirely. They sounded like whispers at first, then grew clearer, telling fragments of stories we couldn't quite piece together.

"Did you hear that?" Chloe asked, leaning over the railing.

We nodded. The voices seemed to come from all directions at once, weaving tales of travelers and crossings, of islands that appeared and disappeared, of ferries that sailed between worlds instead of shores.

illustrations.description: The ferry boat emerging from thick fog on Mirror Lake, with passengers boarding. Chloe, with short wavy strawberry-blonde hair, light skin, and blue-gray eyes, stands excitedly near the gangplank. Enchanted forest style with glowing mushrooms along the barely visible shoreline, fairy lights dancing in the mist, mystical atmosphere with rich greens and purples, golden sparkles throughout the fog, Studio Ghibli inspired magical atmosphere with detailed ethereal elements surrounding the boat.
illustrations.alt: Ferry boat in thick fog with passengers boarding

chapters.title: The Whispering Waters
chapters.content:
Captain Morrison seemed unbothered by the voices, but we watched his expression change as they grew louder and more distinct. He adjusted his compass twice, then three times, frowning at the needle that spun without finding true north.

"Probably just echoes from the shore," he muttered, but his voice carried uncertainty.

The fog grew thicker as we moved deeper into the lake. It wasn't ordinary mist—it shimmered with hints of silver and gold, and sometimes we glimpsed shapes moving within it that looked almost like people.

Chloe pressed closer to the railing, her eyes wide with wonder. "Listen," she said. "They're telling stories about us."

We strained our ears and realized she was right. The voices spoke of a cheerful girl with strawberry-blonde hair who would play a vital role in something important. They mentioned a group of children who traveled together, whose bond would be tested by separation and doubt.

"That's impossible," whispered Jenny Patterson. "How could they know who we are?"

But the voices continued, describing each passenger with unsettling accuracy—Mrs. Chen's love of jasmine tea, Mr. Bramble's collection of walking sticks, even Captain Morrison's thirty years of ferry service.

"These are just coincidences," Mrs. Chen said firmly, though her hands shook as she poured tea from her thermos. "Lake fog can play tricks on the mind."

Chloe, however, seemed enchanted rather than frightened. She called out to the fog, "Hello! Who are you? Why are you telling stories about us?"

The voices paused, and for a moment the only sound was the ferry's engine and the gentle lapping of water against the hull. Then they resumed, but now they spoke directly to Chloe, welcoming her by name and inviting her to listen more carefully.

"Don't encourage them," warned Mr. Bramble, but his voice was gentle. "Strange things happen on this lake when the fog rolls in. Best to stay together and keep our wits about us."

Yet we found ourselves drawn to the voices despite the warning. They told beautiful stories—tales of courage and friendship, of mysteries solved and families reunited. Each story felt true in the moment we heard it, though details seemed to shift if we tried to remember them exactly.

illustrations.description: Chloe with short wavy strawberry-blonde hair, light skin, and blue-gray eyes leaning over the ferry railing, calling out to mysterious shapes visible in the shimmering fog. Other passengers gather behind her with worried expressions. The fog glows with silver and gold hints, containing ethereal figures. Enchanted forest style with glowing mushrooms reflecting off the water, fairy lights swirling through the mist, mystical creatures barely visible in the fog, rich greens and purples, golden sparkles, Studio Ghibli inspired magical atmosphere with detailed foliage-like patterns in the mist.
illustrations.alt: Girl calling out to mysterious shapes in glowing fog from ferry deck

chapters.title: Day Two: Lost in Stories
chapters.content:
We woke to find ourselves still surrounded by fog, though none of us remembered falling asleep. The ferry rocked gently at what appeared to be a small dock, but when we peered through the mist, we couldn't see any island or shore.

"Where are we?" asked Tom Patterson, his voice tight with worry.

Captain Morrison checked his instruments, his frown deepening. "According to my charts, we should have reached Beacon Island hours ago. But this... this isn't any dock I know."

The voices had grown stronger overnight, no longer whispering but speaking in normal tones from every direction. They told contradictory stories now—some claiming we had arrived safely at our destination, others insisting we were lost at sea, still others suggesting we had never left the original dock at all.

"My compass spins like a top," the Captain admitted. "And my radio picks up nothing but static."

Chloe seemed less cheerful than usual, though she tried to maintain her optimistic spirit. "Maybe we're exactly where we're supposed to be," she suggested, but her voice wavered with uncertainty.

The problem wasn't just our location—it was the stories themselves. Each voice told a different version of events, and we found ourselves believing whichever one we heard most recently. According to one voice, we had been rescued by a passing yacht and were safely on our way home. Another insisted we had discovered a magical island full of treasures. A third claimed we were heroes who had saved the entire lake from an ancient curse.

"They can't all be true," Mrs. Chen said, though she sounded less certain than before.

"But they feel true when we hear them," added Jenny Patterson.

We realized this was our real problem: the voices didn't just tell stories—they made us believe them completely while we listened. Only when we concentrated very hard could we remember that we had heard different, conflicting versions just moments before.

Chloe wandered toward the bow of the ferry, drawn by a particularly compelling voice that spoke of finding the way home. We called after her, but the fog seemed to muffle our words. She moved farther away, following the story that promised to lead her to safety.

"Chloe, wait!" we shouted, but she was already disappearing into the thick mist, following a voice that might or might not be telling the truth.

illustrations.description: The ferry docked at a mysterious, barely visible dock shrouded in thick fog. Chloe, with short wavy strawberry-blonde hair, light skin, and blue-gray eyes, walks toward the bow following floating lights in the mist while other passengers call after her with worried gestures. Multiple ethereal voices are represented as glowing wisps around the boat. Enchanted forest style with glowing mushrooms growing on the dock posts, fairy lights leading Chloe forward, mystical creatures watching from the fog, rich greens and purples, golden sparkles, Studio Ghibli inspired magical atmosphere with detailed foliage emerging from the mist.
illustrations.alt: Girl following mysterious lights away from ferry while others call after her

chapters.title: The Separation
chapters.content:
Panic spread through our group as we lost sight of Chloe in the fog. The voices continued their stories, but now they seemed different—less welcoming, more insistent. Some claimed Chloe had found her way to safety, others insisted she was in terrible danger, still others suggested she had never existed at all.

"We have to find her," declared Mrs. Chen, setting down her thermos with determination.

"But which way?" asked Mr. Bramble. "The fog is so thick we can barely see our own hands."

Captain Morrison tried starting the ferry's engine, but it sputtered and died. "Something's wrong with the motor," he admitted. "We're not going anywhere until this fog lifts."

We called Chloe's name over and over, but our voices seemed to disappear into the mist without carrying far. Meanwhile, the story-voices grew louder, each one claiming to know exactly where she had gone and what had happened to her.

"She's safe on Beacon Island," said one voice.

"She fell into the lake and needs rescue," insisted another.

"She found a secret passage to another world," suggested a third.

"There is no Chloe," whispered a fourth. "You imagined her."

That last voice made our hearts stop. We looked at each other with growing horror. Could it be true? Had we somehow invented our friend? But no—we remembered her strawberry-blonde hair, her cheerful laugh, the way she stumbled over her own feet when excited.

"Don't listen to that one," urged Jenny Patterson. "I remember Chloe perfectly. She was real. She is real."

But doubt crept into our minds like the fog itself. The voices were so convincing, and each story felt completely true while we heard it. How could we tell which voice spoke the truth and which ones lied?

"This is exactly what they want," realized Mr. Bramble, tapping his walking stick thoughtfully against the deck. "They want us to doubt what we know. To believe their stories instead of our own memories."

We spent the rest of the day searching the small dock and the few feet of shoreline we could see through the fog. But Chloe was nowhere to be found, and the voices continued their contradictory tales, each one more convincing than the last.

illustrations.description: The group of passengers searching frantically on the mysterious dock, calling out into the thick fog. Speech bubbles show their calls for "Chloe!" while swirling mists contain conflicting story-voices represented as different colored wisps and shadows. The ferry sits disabled in the background. Enchanted forest style with glowing mushrooms along the dock, fairy lights creating confusing paths in multiple directions, mystical creatures watching from the shadows, rich greens and purples, golden sparkles, Studio Ghibli inspired magical atmosphere with detailed foliage and twisted trees emerging from the fog.
illustrations.alt: Group searching for missing friend on foggy dock with mysterious voices swirling around

chapters.title: Chloe Alone
chapters.content:
Meanwhile, Chloe wandered through the fog alone, following a path that seemed to form beneath her feet as she walked. The voice that had led her away from the ferry spoke gently, reassuringly, telling her she was almost home.

But other voices whispered different stories. Some said she was walking deeper into danger, others that she was moving in circles, still others that her friends had abandoned her deliberately.

At first, Chloe tried to sort through the conflicting tales, but the fog seemed to press against her thoughts, making it hard to concentrate. Each voice sounded so reasonable, so certain of its version of events.

"My family is worried about me," she said aloud, trying to anchor herself to what she knew was true.

"Your family forgot about you long ago," whispered one voice.

"Your family is safe at home, wondering why you haven't called," suggested another.

"You have no family—you're all alone in the world," hissed a third.

Chloe stopped walking and sat down on what felt like a large stone. Her usual cheerfulness wavered as confusion and fear crept in. Without her friends nearby, it was much harder to resist the voices' influence.

But then she remembered something important. Before the ferry trip, her little brother had given her a smooth pebble from their garden, claiming it was "lucky." She pulled it from her pocket now, running her fingers over its familiar surface.

The pebble felt real and solid in a way the voices didn't. It reminded her of home, of her brother's gap-toothed grin, of her mother's pancakes on Saturday mornings. These memories belonged to her, not to the voices in the fog.

"You're trying to confuse me," she said aloud, standing up with new determination. "But I know what's real."

The voices grew more insistent, layering story upon story until they nearly drowned out her thoughts. But Chloe held tight to her pebble and to her own memories, using them like a compass to navigate through the maze of false tales.

Slowly, carefully, she began making her way back toward where she thought the ferry might be, guided not by the voices but by her own sense of truth.

illustrations.description: Chloe, with short wavy strawberry-blonde hair, light skin, and blue-gray eyes, sitting alone on a moss-covered stone in the thick fog, holding a small pebble while multiple colored wisps representing different voices swirl around her. Her expression shows determination despite being surrounded by confusion. A faint path glows beneath her feet. Enchanted forest style with glowing mushrooms providing the only light, fairy lights dancing confusingly in multiple directions, mystical creatures lurking in the shadows, rich greens and purples, golden sparkles, Studio Ghibli inspired magical atmosphere with detailed foliage and twisted branches reaching through the fog.
illustrations.alt: Girl sitting alone in fog holding a pebble while mysterious voices swirl around her

chapters.title: Day Three: The Choice to Believe
chapters.content:
Dawn brought no lessening of the fog, but we woke with clearer minds. During the night, we had each held onto something real—Mrs. Chen her thermos, Mr. Bramble his walking stick, the Patterson twins each other's hands. These anchors had helped us resist the voices' most confusing stories.

"I've been thinking," said Captain Morrison. "Thirty years on this lake, and I've never seen fog behave like this. It's not natural—which means it might not follow natural rules."

"What do you mean?" asked Tom Patterson.

"I mean maybe we don't need my compass or my charts to find our way. Maybe we need to trust what we know is true instead of what we're told."

The voices immediately began protesting, offering new stories about dangerous waters and hidden rocks, about the impossibility of navigation without instruments. But we were learning to hear the difference between their tales and our own knowledge.

"We came here together," said Mrs. Chen firmly. "All of us, including Chloe. That's what I know is true."

"And Chloe is cheerful and clumsy and has strawberry-blonde hair," added Jenny Patterson. "I remember her tripping over the gangplank when we boarded."

"She was excited about the fog," we all recalled together. "She said it was like sailing into a fairy tale."

The voices tried to convince us these were false memories, planted by the fog itself. They spun elaborate explanations about how isolation could create imaginary companions, how stress could make people believe in things that never existed.

But we held tight to our shared recollections, comparing details and finding they matched perfectly. The voices' stories kept changing, but our memories remained constant.

"If we can trust our memories," reasoned Mr. Bramble, "then we can trust that Chloe is real and that she needs our help."

"But how do we find her in all this fog?" asked Captain Morrison.

"The same way we remembered her," we realized. "By choosing what to believe."

Instead of listening to the conflicting voices, we began calling out our own story—the true story of our trip, of Chloe's excitement, of our friendship and concern. We spoke loudly and clearly, drowning out the whispered tales with our own certain knowledge.

illustrations.description: The group of passengers standing together on the ferry deck, holding their personal anchor items (thermos, walking stick, etc.) while speaking in unison. Their words form visible golden letters in the fog that push back against the swirling dark wisps of the false voices. Captain Morrison looks determined at the wheel. Enchanted forest style with glowing mushrooms providing warm light, fairy lights beginning to arrange in more orderly patterns, mystical creatures stepping back from the group's united stance, rich greens and purples, golden sparkles forming protective barriers, Studio Ghibli inspired magical atmosphere with detailed foliage beginning to part.
illustrations.alt: Group of people standing together speaking truth that pushes back the fog

chapters.title: The Power of True Stories
chapters.content:
As we spoke our truth aloud, something remarkable happened. The fog began to thin around us, not disappearing entirely but becoming less oppressive. The voices grew quieter, their stories less convincing.

And then we heard it—Chloe's voice calling back to us through the mist.

"I'm here!" she shouted. "I'm trying to find my way back!"

We called to her, guiding her with our voices while the story-voices tried to interfere. They suggested she was an echo, a trick of the fog, anything except our real friend returning to us.

But we knew better now. We kept calling, and slowly Chloe's voice grew nearer. When she finally emerged from the fog, stumbling slightly in her eagerness, we saw her exactly as we remembered—short wavy strawberry-blonde hair, light skin, blue-gray eyes bright with relief and determination.

"You're real," she gasped, hugging each of us in turn.

"So are you," we replied. "We never doubted it. Well, not for long."

Chloe showed us the pebble her brother had given her. "I held onto this," she explained. "It reminded me of what was true. The voices told so many stories, but this pebble never changed."

"That's exactly what we did," said Mrs. Chen, holding up her thermos. "We held onto real things and real memories."

The fog continued to lift as we shared our experiences. We realized that the voices weren't evil—they were simply stories without anchors, tales that had become separated from truth and now drifted like the fog itself, attaching to anyone who would listen.

"But why did they affect us so strongly?" asked Tom Patterson.

"Because stories have power," explained Mr. Bramble. "Especially when we're uncertain or afraid. They offer us explanations, even false ones, when we desperately want to understand what's happening."

Captain Morrison nodded thoughtfully. "I've heard tales of fog like this before—old sailors' stories about mists that carried voices. I always thought they were just legends."

"Maybe they were legends that became real," suggested Chloe. "Maybe that's what happens when stories drift too far from truth."

illustrations.description: Chloe, with short wavy strawberry-blonde hair, light skin, and blue-gray eyes, emerging from the thinning fog with arms outstretched toward her friends who rush to embrace her. She holds the small pebble in one hand while golden light radiates from their reunion. The oppressive fog is lifting, revealing glimpses of the actual lake and distant shores. Enchanted forest style with glowing mushrooms celebrating along the dock, fairy lights dancing joyfully, mystical creatures gathering peacefully, rich greens and purples becoming clearer, golden sparkles swirling in celebration, Studio Ghibli inspired magical atmosphere with detailed foliage now clearly visible.
illustrations.alt: Friends reuniting as fog lifts around them on the ferry dock

chapters.title: Finding the True Path
chapters.content:
With Chloe back among us and our understanding clearer, we faced a choice. The fog was lifting slowly, but we could see multiple paths through the remaining mist—some leading toward what might be Beacon Island, others toward possible escape routes, still others deeper into the mysterious waters.

The voices still whispered their conflicting directions, but we had learned to distinguish between their stories and our own judgment.

"Which way should we go?" asked Jenny Patterson.

Captain Morrison checked his compass, which had begun working properly again as the fog thinned. "According to this, Beacon Island should be due north. But I can see paths in three different directions, and they all look equally real."

"Maybe that's the final test," suggested Chloe, her natural optimism returning. "Maybe we have to choose based on what we believe, not on what we're told."

We discussed our options carefully, weighing what we knew against what the voices claimed. One path looked easier but felt wrong somehow—too convenient, too much like the simple solutions the voices had offered. Another looked dangerous but familiar, like routes we had traveled before in different circumstances.

The third path seemed ordinary, neither particularly easy nor especially difficult. It reminded us of the original ferry route we had planned, before the fog changed everything.

"I think we should take the ordinary path," said Mrs. Chen. "It feels true."

"But what if it leads us astray?" worried Tom Patterson.

"Then we'll figure out what to do next," replied Mr. Bramble. "That's what real people do—they make choices with incomplete information and adjust as they learn more."

Chloe nodded enthusiastically. "That's so different from the voices' stories. They always claimed to know exactly what would happen, but real life isn't that certain."

We voted and chose the ordinary path—not because a voice told us to, but because it felt consistent with our own experience and judgment.

Captain Morrison restarted the ferry's engine, which purred to life without difficulty. As we began moving through the clearing fog, we could finally see where we were going.

illustrations.description: The ferry at a crossroads in the clearing fog, with three distinct paths visible through the mist - one appearing too easy with golden light, one dark and dangerous, and one ordinary-looking middle path. Chloe, with short wavy strawberry-blonde hair, light skin, and blue-gray eyes, points toward the ordinary path while the group gathers around Captain Morrison at the wheel making their decision together. Enchanted forest style with glowing mushrooms lining each path differently, fairy lights showing the way forward on the chosen route, mystical creatures watching respectfully from the sidelines, rich greens and purples, golden sparkles, Studio Ghibli inspired magical atmosphere with detailed foliage framing each pathway.
illustrations.alt: Ferry choosing between three paths through clearing fog

chapters.title: Clear Waters
chapters.content:
The ordinary path led us exactly where we hoped it would—to Beacon Island, where the lighthouse stood tall and welcoming against the clearing sky. The fog continued to dissipate as we approached, revealing the lake's true beauty and the worried faces of our families waiting on the dock.

"There you are!" called Chloe's mother, waving frantically. "We've been so worried—you were supposed to arrive hours ago!"

"We got lost in the fog," Captain Morrison explained as he expertly guided the ferry to the dock. "But we found our way."

As our families embraced us with relief and joy, we realized something important. The story-voices had faded completely, but we could still hear real voices all around us—people sharing genuine experiences, expressing true emotions, offering authentic help.

"How did you know to choose that path?" asked Chloe's father.

"We learned to tell the difference between stories that help and stories that confuse," Chloe explained, still clutching her lucky pebble. "Real stories connect us to each other and to what's true. False ones separate us and make us doubt what we know."

Mrs. Chen poured fresh tea for everyone, sharing it generously as she always did. Mr. Bramble told the lighthouse keeper about our adventure, stick firmly in hand as he gestured. The Patterson twins chattered excitedly to their parents about what they had learned.

As we explored Beacon Island together, we noticed that people were constantly sharing stories—about the lighthouse's history, about other visitors who had come before, about their own experiences with the lake's unusual weather. But these stories felt different from the voices in the fog.

"How can you tell which stories to trust?" asked Tom Patterson.

We thought about this carefully. "Real stories admit when they don't know everything," we realized. "And they encourage you to think for yourself instead of just believing whatever you're told."

"Also," added Chloe, "true stories connect you to other people and real experiences. False ones make you feel isolated and confused."

The lighthouse keeper, an elderly woman with kind eyes, nodded approvingly. "You children have learned something very valuable. In my forty years here, I've seen many people get lost in that fog—not just physically, but mentally. The ones who find their way out are always those who hold onto what they know is real."

illustrations.description: The ferry safely docked at Beacon Island with the lighthouse standing proudly in the background under clearing skies. Families embrace their children on the sunny dock, with Chloe, showing short wavy strawberry-blonde hair, light skin, and blue-gray eyes, hugging her parents while holding the pebble. The fog has completely lifted, revealing the beautiful lake and island. People share stories around the lighthouse keeper. Enchanted forest style with glowing mushrooms now peaceful around the island, fairy lights celebrating in the trees, mystical creatures watching benevolently from the forest, rich greens and purples in full sunlight, golden sparkles dancing in celebration, Studio Ghibli inspired magical atmosphere with detailed foliage and flowers blooming around the lighthouse.
illustrations.alt: Families reuniting on sunny dock with lighthouse and cleared skies

metadata.lessonPlan: Discuss how to evaluate the reliability of different sources of information and the importance of checking facts against multiple sources.

coverPrompt.description: A ferry boat floating in thick, luminescent fog on a dark lake, with mysterious glowing lights visible through the mist. Chloe, a girl with short wavy strawberry-blonde hair, light skin, and blue-gray eyes, stands at the boat's railing looking into the fog. Enchanted forest style with glowing mushrooms visible along distant shores, fairy lights dancing in the mist, mystical atmosphere with rich greens and purples, golden sparkles throughout the fog, Studio Ghibli inspired magical atmosphere with detailed ethereal elements.
coverPrompt.alt: A girl on a ferry surrounded by mystical fog and glowing lights

questions.question: What did Chloe use to help her remember what was true while lost in the fog?
choices: A lucky pebble from her brother
questions.explanation: Chloe held onto the lucky pebble her brother gave her, which reminded her of real memories and helped her resist the false stories in the fog.

questions.question: How did the group learn to distinguish between true and false stories?
choices: They asked Captain Morrison for advice
They held onto real objects and trusted their shared memories
questions.explanation: The group held onto real objects (like Mrs. Chen's thermos and Mr. Bramble's walking stick) and compared their shared memories to anchor themselves to truth.

questions.question: What made the voices in the fog particularly confusing?
choices: They spoke in a foreign language
They told contradictory stories that each seemed true while being heard
They were too quiet to understand
questions.explanation: The voices told conflicting stories, and each one felt completely true while the characters were listening to it, making it difficult to determine what was real.

questions.question: Why did the group choose the 'ordinary' path when three routes were available?
choices: A voice told them it was safest
It felt true and consistent with their experience
Captain Morrison's compass pointed that way
questions.explanation: The group chose the ordinary path because it felt true and matched their own judgment, rather than being too easy or following what voices told them.

questions.question: What was the main difference between the fog voices and the real stories shared on Beacon Island?
choices: Real stories admitted uncertainty and encouraged independent thinking
Real stories only came from adults
questions.explanation: Unlike the fog voices, real stories acknowledged when they didn't know everything and encouraged people to think for themselves rather than just believe what they were told.

questions.question: How long had Captain Morrison been crossing Mirror Lake?
questions.explanation: Captain Morrison mentioned that he had been crossing Mirror Lake for thirty years, making the unusual fog behavior particularly strange to him.

questions.question: What happened to Captain Morrison's compass in the fog?
choices: It spun without finding true north
questions.explanation: The fog caused Captain Morrison's compass to spin like a top without finding true north, making normal navigation impossible.

questions.question: According to the lighthouse keeper, what helps people find their way out of the confusing fog?
choices: Holding onto what they know is real
questions.explanation: The lighthouse keeper explained that people who successfully navigate the fog are those who hold onto what they know is real, just as the children had learned to do.
